title: Why 'investing isn't for me' is a story
subtitle: A look at where the exclusion belief comes from and what it costs in compound time.
excerpt: A reading from the Day 40 theme. The belief that investing belongs to wealthier, more qualified, or more financially sophisticated people is one of the most common stories in the library — and one of the most expensive ones to carry.
sourceNotes: Draws on the Day 40 theme of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Investing isn't for others — it's for you." Supporting context from behavioural finance research on investor identity and the access gap (including work by Vanguard's Centre for Investor Research and Fidelity Investments on first-generation investors). The belief analysis draws on the authors' clinical observations in the tapping program.

practice.whenToUse: Read when the word 'investing' triggers a feeling of exclusion — that it requires more money, knowledge, or confidence than you currently hold.

content.text: There is a version of the investing story that goes: I will start when I have more money. Or: when I understand it better. Or: when I am the kind of person who does that. All three versions share the same structure — a prerequisite that keeps moving every time you get close to it. The money is not enough yet. The knowledge is not enough yet. The person is not enough yet. And so the starting date stays in the future.
This reading is about that pattern: where it comes from, what it costs, and what it would take to interrupt it.
Where the story starts

Most people learn their money beliefs before they earn money. The household they grew up in had a relationship with investing — or didn't. In households where investing was a topic at the table, children absorbed it as a normal thing adults do. In households where it wasn't, the silence was its own message: this is not something we do. Not because anyone said so, but because no one ever modelled it.

Add to that the way investing has been publicly framed: television portrayals of traders in suits, newspaper coverage of market crashes, the jargon-heavy language of financial services that has historically been written for an assumed reader who already owns a significant amount of money. The signal, received cumulatively, is clear: this is for people who are already in the room. If you are not already in the room, you do not belong.
The story 'investing isn't for me' is not stupidity or irrationality. It is a reasonable conclusion drawn from the evidence that was available. The problem is that the evidence was incomplete.
The three most common versions
The exclusion story usually arrives in one of three forms.
Not enough money.

This version says: I need a lump sum to begin. I need to be debt-free first. I need to have cleared the credit card, topped up the emergency fund, bought the house. The list of prerequisites is long, and each item completed reveals another item underneath it. The threshold for 'enough to start' keeps receding.

The practical reality is that modern investment platforms allow you to start with tens of pounds, not thousands. A small monthly contribution into a low-cost index fund does not need a prerequisite list. The barrier to entry as an actual mechanic is low. The barrier as a felt sense is often enormous.
Not enough knowledge.

This version says: I don't know enough about how it works. I need to understand the markets first. I should do more research. It presents as responsible preparation; in practice it becomes an indefinite delay. The research never ends because the goal of feeling sufficiently prepared is not anchored to anything.

Most research on individual investors finds that active stock-picking by non-professionals underperforms passive index investment over the long run. The people who spend the most time researching individual stocks often do worse than people who buy and hold a broad market fund and do very little. Knowledge, in the sense of expertise about individual securities, is largely not what determines outcomes for the typical long-term investor.
Wrong type of person.

This version is the subtlest. It doesn't name money or knowledge. It names identity: investors are a type of person, and I am not that type. They are bolder, more financially minded, more comfortable with risk. They probably had a parent who showed them how. They move in circles where this is talked about. I am not from those circles.

This version is the hardest to interrupt because it isn't really about investing. It is about belonging. About whether this particular domain of adult financial life is a space you are allowed to occupy.
What the story costs

The cost of the 'not yet' story is compound time. Compounding works on the length of time money is invested as much as the amount invested. Money invested at thirty compounds for thirty-five years to retirement age. Money invested at forty-five compounds for twenty years. The same monthly amount at the same return rate produces a materially different outcome depending on when the clock started.

The story doesn't cost you the amount you didn't invest. It costs you the growth of the amount you didn't invest across the years you waited. The invisible price is the compound growth that never had the chance to run.

This is not a guilt argument. Guilt is not useful here and the point is not to look back. The point is that the story has a price tag that is easy to miss because the cost is in an account that was never opened, not a transaction that can be seen.
Investing as a skill that builds with doing

The knowledge-prerequisite version of the story assumes that investing is front-loaded: you learn first, then you start. The actual experience of most investors is the opposite. The learning happens by doing. Opening an account, choosing an initial fund, watching what happens when a market drops, noticing your emotional response to a red number and a green one — these experiences produce information that no amount of pre-reading generates.

Starting small is not a consolation prize for people who can't afford to start properly. It is the correct starting point for building the emotional relationship with investing that the knowledge-only approach misses entirely. A small real position teaches you more about your own risk tolerance, patience, and behaviour under uncertainty than a large theoretical one.
Changing the story starts with identity

All three versions of the story are identity stories. They say: investing is for a different kind of person, and that person has more money, more knowledge, or more of the right background than I do. The prerequisite is never just money or knowledge — it is permission.

No amount of market education gives you permission that you haven't given yourself. The identity shift to "I am someone who invests" has to happen at the belief level, not the information level. The tapping and energy statement practices that accompany this reading work on that layer: the 'I am not that type of person' layer that the research articles don't touch.

The strategy question: what to invest in, how much, and when. It matters and there are good resources for it. But it is not the first question. The first question is the one the story keeps you from asking: what if this is for me, starting now, with what I have?
